attributes.stem:
A three-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parent concerned about multiple red-brown spots that have been present on his trunk and limbs for six months. He attends daycare weekly. The spots occasionally become raised and itchy, particularly after a warm bath. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. His immunisations are up to date.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple red-brown macules scattered over his trunk and limbs. When the skin lesions are stroked gently with a tongue depressor, they become raised and erythematous within minutes. There is no hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic features of urticaria pigmentosa, the most common form of cutaneous mastocytosis in children. The key diagnostic feature described is Darier's sign - the development of localised urticaria following stroking of the lesions. This occurs due to mechanical stimulation causing mast cell degranulation.

Mastocytosis can be divided into cutaneous mastocytosis which only affects the skin, systemic mastocytosis (with non-specific symptoms of fatigue and pain) and mast cell sarcoma. There is an association with somatic mutations of the KIT gene however there are more variants which are not yet clear of the cause. Cutaneous mastcytosis is further divided into uricaria pigmentosa, diffuse cutaneous mastocytosis and mastocytoma of skin.

Urticaria pigmentosa typically presents in early childhood (less than 2 years old) with multiple red-brown macules that become urticarial when stroked, and occurs on the scalp, face, trunk and limbs. Other triggers for mast cell degranulation include heat, friction, and emotional stress. Some patients may experience systemic symptoms such as flushing, pruritus, and gastrointestinal symptoms due to mast cell mediator release. Prognosis is generally favourable and 7 out of 10 children will have significant improvement by age ten years.

Juvenile xanthogranuloma presents as firm, yellow-orange papules or nodules that do not demonstrate Darier's sign. These lesions typically appear in infancy and may spontaneously resolve.

Café au lait macules are light brown, well-circumscribed macules that are stable and do not become urticarial when stroked. Multiple café au lait macules may be associated with neurofibromatosis type 1.

Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation occurs following inflammation or injury to the skin. These macules are stable and do not demonstrate Darier's sign. There would typically be a history of preceding skin inflammation.

Scabies may present with multiple raised itchy lesions on the body and tend to develop in a line. It also may be present on the arms and leg. However the itch tends to be intense, it is highly contagious in the same household and does not demonstrate Darier's sign either.
learningPoints: Darier's sign (urticaria following stroking of the skin) is pathognomonic for cutaneous mastocytosis.
The most common form of cutaneous mastocytosis in children is urticaria pigmentosa, presenting as red-brown macules.
references: Oakley A, Yung A.  Maculopapular cutaneousmastocytosis. DermNet NZ.  2014.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/maculopapular-cutaneous-mastocytosis

Pardanani A, Systemic mastocytosis in adults: 2023 update on diagnosis, risk stratification and management. American Journalo f Hematology. 2023: 98 (7).  Available at: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/ajh.26962#
Royal Children's Hospital Dermatology department. Mastocytosis. Royal Children Hospital. 2018.  Available at: https://www.rch.org.au/kidsinfo/fact_sheets/Mastocytosis/
